title: The Doors of Perception — Blake and the Cleansing of Vision
author: William Blake

body.text: Blake breaks the moral architecture of the world. Heaven and Hell become forces inside perception — Reason and Energy, restraint and desire, law and fire. His aim is to free vision from the prison of inherited categories. The world is already infinite. Perception has narrowed itself until it sees fragments through acrack.
Once meek, and in aperilous path, Good is Heaven. Evil is Hell.

Blake'smethod is violence against dead perception. The poem shocks — sets Heaven and Hell in motion, breaks their conventional meanings, forces the reader to feel energy where they expected evil. He tears open the inherited world so perception can become infinite again. The first act is cleansing.

body.caption: Rintrah roaring — prophetic fire entering the burdened air.

body.text: All Bibles or sacred codes have been the causes of errors. Energy is Eternal Delight.

Blake'sbody is aportion of soul — one of the strongest anti-dualist gestures in visionary literature. The body has been misperceived. Desire, energy, sensation, and imagination are obstacles — they become distorted when governed by fear and dead law. The body is imaginal when perceived as soul'ssurface.
If the doors of perception were cleansed, every thing would appear to man as it is, infinite.

This is Blake'scentral sentence for the Imaginarium. The doors are already there. Perception has the capacity for infinity already built in — it has been crusted over by habit. Cleansing is removal. Blake does not add new spiritual organs. He strips away the dead layers until the infinite shows through the ordinary.

body.caption: The doors cleansed — the infinite appearing through the ordinary.

body.text: Blake'spath is the shortest of all the imaginal traditions. There is no ladder, no technique, no stages. Clean the doors. See what is already there. The infinite does not arrive — it was always present, crusted over by what we call normal vision.
